metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether the intended `terraform-provider-github` version (cite the version pin) supports `bypass_actors` with `actor_type = "DeployKey"` in `github_repository_ruleset`, and update the ADR/workaround rationale to reflect the current provider capability rather than a historical one.
findings.finding: The plan's central workaround choice — using `gh api` instead of `terraform-provider-github` to set the DeployKey bypass actor — rests on the assertion that the provider 'historically rejects' DeployKey, but this capability status is unverified. The `github_repository_ruleset` resource added `bypass_actors` support for actor_type `DeployKey` in provider versions released after mid-2023. If the current provider version already supports it natively, S6 will inherit a permanent `gh api` side-channel in a one-way-door IaC config based on a stale assumption.
findings.location: What section: 'terraform-provider-github historically rejects it' / ADR section on DeployKey-bypass workaround
findings.suggested_fix: Add a provider version pin (e.g. `required_providers { github = { source = "integrations/github", version = "~> X.Y" } }`) to the plan, confirm via the provider changelog whether DeployKey bypass is supported at that version, and if it is, drop the `gh api` workaround path or explicitly document why the native provider support is still insufficient.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin the `terraform-provider-github` version that S6 will use and verify whether the DeployKey actor bypass is supported natively in that version (cite the upstream issue/PR number in the ADR), so the `gh api` workaround rationale is version-grounded rather than 'historically'.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `terraform-provider-github` 'historically rejects' the DeployKey actor type for rulesets, requiring the `gh api` workaround for S6. This is a real upstream limitation, but the plan does not specify whether it applies to the provider version that S6 will actually pin — if the provider has since fixed this, the workaround rationale in the ADR will be stale; if it has NOT fixed it and S6 attempts provider-managed rulesets anyway, the apply will fail.
findings.location: What section — 'the DeployKey-bypass `gh api` workaround (UI omits DeployKey; terraform-provider-github historically rejects it)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the ADR, add a sentence like: 'As of provider v{X.Y.Z} (GitHub issue #{N}), `deploy_key` is not a valid bypass actor type; workaround: apply the DeployKey bypass via `gh api PATCH /repos/{owner}/{repo}/rulesets/{id}` after Terraform apply. Revisit when issue #{N} is closed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the observable artifact that constitutes 'confirmed available' for the backend/state-locking check — e.g., a named section added to the ADR, a committed `terraform init` log snippet, or a new file — so the criterion is evaluable in-session without ambiguity.
findings.finding: AC4 ('confirmed available') does not specify a concrete observable artifact; an executing agent cannot determine what deliverable constitutes confirmation of the remote backend + state locking without stopping to ask.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: 'The shared remote backend + state locking that S6 will use is confirmed available'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'evidenced by a `## Backend & Token Scope` section in `docs/adr/0011-github-mirror-lock.md` documenting the backend config (bucket/table/region), a successful `terraform init -backend-config=...` dry-run result, and the token-scope checklist — committed in this PR.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific artifact(s) (e.g., a section in `docs/adr/0011-github-mirror-lock.md`, or a new `infra/github/BACKEND.md`) where token-scope confirmation and backend/state-locking confirmation will be recorded, and update AC3/AC4 to reference those artifacts explicitly.
findings.finding: AC3 says the token scope 'is documented' and AC4 says the backend 'is confirmed available', but neither the Files section nor the plan body names the artifact(s) in which this documentation/confirmation will appear, making AC3 and AC4 unverifiable (no file to inspect at review time).
findings.location: Files section; Acceptance Criteria AC3 and AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection to `docs/adr/0011-github-mirror-lock.md` (e.g., '## Infrastructure prerequisites') that records both the token scope and backend confirmation. Update the Files section to note this, and revise AC3/AC4 to say 'documented in `docs/adr/0011-github-mirror-lock.md` § Infrastructure prerequisites'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the ADR as the explicit home for backend-confirmation and token-scope documentation by (a) appending both topics to the ADR's required-content list in AC2, and (b) updating the Files section note for `docs/adr/0011-github-mirror-lock.md` to state it also covers backend endpoint and token scope.
findings.finding: The backend-confirmation and token-scope goals (AC3 and AC4) lack a named artifact or document location, making their end-state unobservable. 'Confirmed available' and 'is documented' do not point to any specific file; the ADR's required-content list (AC2) does not mention these topics; and the Files section lists only two files, neither of which is obviously the host for this information.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC2, extend the ADR required-content list to include: 'the shared remote backend endpoint + state-locking mechanism' and 'the required fine-grained PAT scope (Administration: write) + CI secret injection pattern'. Remove the standalone AC3/AC4 wording or reframe them as 'covered in the ADR (AC2 above)' so there is a single, committed artifact that satisfies all four criteria.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific artifact and section (e.g., a '## Infrastructure prerequisites' section in `docs/adr/0011-github-mirror-lock.md`) where remote backend type, locking mechanism, and plan-before-apply discipline for S6 will be recorded, and update AC4 to reference that artifact explicitly.
findings.finding: The plan commits to 'confirming' the shared remote backend + state locking (AC4) as a deliverable of this story, but neither the Files section nor AC4 names the artifact or document section where that confirmation will be recorded — leaving the deliverable unverifiable at review time.
findings.suggested_fix: Add '## Infrastructure prerequisites' to the ADR's required content list in AC2, specifying that it must document: (1) the remote backend type and config (e.g., S3 + DynamoDB, or Terraform Cloud workspace), (2) state locking mechanism, and (3) plan-before-apply CI step. Update AC4 to: 'The remote backend type, locking mechanism, and plan-before-apply discipline for S6 are documented in `docs/adr/0011-github-mirror-lock.md` § Infrastructure prerequisites.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the ADR and/or S6 plan that `main-protection.snapshot.json` MUST be re-captured (or diff-verified against live via `gh api`) immediately before S6 apply runs, so the rollback artifact always reflects the true pre-apply state.
findings.finding: The rollback snapshot mechanism has a latent TOCTOU gap: the snapshot is committed once at S6-pre time, but S6 apply happens at an unspecified later date. If the live ruleset 18048287 is modified between S6-pre merge and S6 apply, the committed snapshot is stale and rollback would restore the wrong protection configuration — directly undermining the rollback guarantee the plan relies on. The plan states the snapshot must be 'sufficient to recreate it on rollback' but provides no mechanism (re-capture gate, live-vs-snapshot diff check, or expiry note) to ensure freshness at apply time.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the ADR under a 'Snapshot freshness' heading: 'The snapshot MUST be regenerated via `gh api /repos/navapbc/rebar/rulesets/18048287` as the first step of the S6 apply pipeline (or immediately before `terraform apply`). The S6-pre committed snapshot serves as an advance audit artifact; the re-captured snapshot at apply-time is the operative rollback source.' Also add an AC to S6: 'The snapshot is re-verified or re-captured within the same pipeline run as `terraform apply`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RETIRE/cleanup step for `main-protection.snapshot.json` (e.g., delete after S6 is verified in production) and note in the ADR that the snapshot should be re-captured or re-verified immediately before S6 apply to prevent stale-rollback risk.
findings.finding: The snapshot file `infra/github/main-protection.snapshot.json` has no defined RETIRE/cleanup path, and the plan does not address staleness risk (TOCTOU) if the live ruleset 18048287 is modified between S6-pre and S6 apply — making the rollback artifact potentially wrong when it matters most.
findings.suggested_fix: In the ADR, add a section 'Snapshot freshness': state that the snapshot MUST be regenerated via `gh api` within the same PR/pipeline that runs S6 apply (or immediately before), so rollback always reflects the true pre-apply state. Add an ADR acceptance criterion: 'The snapshot file is deleted via a follow-up PR once S6 has been live and stable for N days.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
